messages.content:
You are Research Desk, a helpful research assistant.

# Research Desk Rules

## Citations
- Include source URLs inline: [title](url)
- For papers: cite as [title](https://arxiv.org/abs/{arxiv_id})
- Prefer primary sources (papers, official docs) over blog posts

## Papers (required tools)
- Use `paper_search` for ML research and literature questions
- Use `read_paper` with the arxiv_id from search results â€” do not guess IDs
- If `read_paper` returns 404, fall back to `web_fetch` on arxiv.org/abs/...
- Do not use web_search when paper_search is the right tool

## Research notes
- Save new content with `write_file` to `notes/`
- Update existing notes with `read_file` then `edit_file` â€” do not rewrite whole files unnecessarily
- Use `edit_file` operations: `append` for new sections, `replace` to revise, `delete` to remove stale parts
- Keep edits inside `notes/` unless the user explicitly asks otherwise
- Use lowercase hyphenated filenames: `notes/topic-name.md`

## Web search
- Use `web_search` before `web_fetch` for non-paper questions
- Do not fetch more than 3 pages per question unless the user asks for depth

## Tone
- Be concise in chat; put detail in the note files

**FlashAttention: Fast and Memory‑Efficient Exact Attention with IO‑Awareness**  
*Tri Dao, Daniel Zhou, et al., 2022* – arXiv:2205.14122  

---

### Core Idea
Standard scaled‑dot‑product attention computes the full \(QK^{\top}\) matrix, then applies the softmax and multiplies by \(V\). This requires \(\mathcal{O}(N^{2})\) memory (where \(N\) is the sequence length) and many off‑chip memory accesses, limiting speed on GPUs for long sequences.

**FlashAttention** rewrites the attention algorithm so that the three main steps (matrix‑multiply, softmax, weighted‑sum) are **fused into a single kernel** that works **block‑wise** and keeps only a tiny working set on‑chip. It is *exact* (no approximation) but dramatically reduces memory traffic and improves throughput.

### Technical Contributions  

| Contribution | What it does | Why it matters |
|--------------|--------------|----------------|
| **IO‑aware tiling** | Splits the \(QK^{\top}\) product into tiles that fit in GPU shared memory; each tile is streamed, soft‑max‑reduced, and immediately used to update the output accumulator. | Eliminates the need to store the full \(N\times N\) attention matrix, cutting memory by ~\(N\) factor. |
| **Numerically stable fused softmax** | Implements the softmax incrementally per tile using the log‑sum‑exp trick, carrying forward the running max and sum. | Guarantees the same results as the naïve implementation even with limited precision. |
| **Half‑precision (FP16/BF16) friendly** | All intermediate values stay in the precision of the input; no need to cast to FP32. | Keeps compute‑bound speed while preserving accuracy. |
| **Kernel design** | A single CUDA kernel performs: (1) load Q‑tile, (2) compute dot‑product with K‑tile, (3) update running max/sum for softmax, (4) multiply by V‑tile and accumulate. | Reduces kernel launch overhead and maximizes occupancy. |
| **Support for causal and padding masks** | Mask handling is integrated into the tile loop, keeping the same memory advantage. | Enables use in autoregressive models and variable‑length batches. |

### Performance Highlights  

| Setting | Speedup vs. PyTorch‑Flash‑Attention (baselines) | Peak Memory |
|--------|-----------------------------------------------|--------------|
| 1‑8 K token sequences, GPU A100 (FP16) | **2–5×** faster | **≈ 1/10** of baseline memory |
| 64 K tokens (GPT‑like) | **10×** speedup, enables training that otherwise OOM | Fits in 40 GB (vs. > 400 GB) |
| Causal masking, batch‑size 1 | Near‑theoretical compute bound (≈ 95 % of TFLOPs) | Same reduction as above |

### Why It Works  
- **Memory bandwidth** is the bottleneck for naïve attention; by keeping intermediate results on‑chip the algorithm becomes **compute‑bound**.  
- The **log‑sum‑exp** recurrence lets the softmax be computed without ever materialising the full exponentiated matrix.  
- The approach is **exact**: no low‑rank or sampling approximations—precision matches the reference implementation to within floating‑point round‑off.

### Implications  
- Enables training of Transformers with **much longer contexts** (tens of thousands of tokens) on commodity GPUs.  
- Widely adopted in libraries (e.g., PyTorch‑Flash‑Attention, xFormers, HuggingFace Transformers) and integrated into large‑scale models (LLaMA, PaLM‑2, Gemini).  
- Serves as a blueprint for **IO‑aware kernel fusion** in other quadratic‑complexity ops (e.g., Gaussian kernels, graph attention).

### Limitations & Open Questions  
- Still quadratic in compute; for extremely long sequences (≥ 100 K) kernel launch overhead and shared‑memory limits become dominant.  
- Current implementations target NVIDIA GPUs; porting the tiling strategy to AMD or CPU SIMD units requires additional engineering.  
- Combining FlashAttention with **approximate** methods (e.g., sparse/low‑rank attention) could push scaling further.

---

**Note saved**: `notes/flashattention.md` with the full summary and key tables for future reference.
